metadata.title: Why the Application Counter Is Lying to You
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine candidate 02. Source chapter: books/the-reallocation-engine/chapters/02-the-reallocation-principle.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, terracotta = the counter / cold channel, dusty blue = the connections channel / return, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: terracotta #D35F43 = the counter / cold channel / feedback; dusty blue #5B7B9C = connections channel / return; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: ON-SCREEN NUMBERS CAPPED AT TWO (card exclusion): ~0.2% cold conversion, ~54% hires via connections. Both inherit the chapter's own [verify] flags (footnotes cite the author's essays, not primary surveys) — see FACTCHECK.md. No 3-3-2 schedule, no freed-hour tree, no conversion table, no worked weekly example.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The most disciplined job search in the room — five hundred meticulous applications a month — is optimizing a number that measures nothing.

card.copy: Why the application counter is lying to you

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] Why the application counter is lying to you

beats.narration_text: Eight a.m. Coffee. Seventeen open tabs. By noon the counter reads thirty; by the end of the day, forty-one. Five days a week. Meticulous.
beats.scene_description: a desk from above: laptop crowded with browser tabs, cooling coffee, a color-coded spreadsheet visible — desaturated print plate on newsprint, slow push toward the screen
beats.image_prompt: overhead photograph of a cluttered desk with a laptop showing many browser tabs and a color-coded spreadsheet, cooling cup of coffee, morning light, desaturated print reproduction on aged newsprint, editorial collage

beats.narration_text: And the counter is the trap. Every submission makes the number go up — immediate, legible, satisfying. The counter going up feels like progress.

production_viz.label: applications sent
production_viz.mechanic: large mono odometer digits tick upward (28 → 41) in terracotta as small submission stamps land beneath; the number is the only thing that moves smoothly
production_viz.note: counter values are scene dressing (the applicant's day from the chapter), not data claims — no axis, no chart

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the odometer ticks up in terracotta

beats.narration_text: Here's what it can't show you: the machine underneath, quietly deleting most of what it's fed.
beats.scene_description: a paper collage machine: application pages feed into a slot at the top, thin shredded strips drift out below — flat print look, no gore, quiet
beats.image_prompt: editorial paper-collage illustration of documents feeding into a slot in a large flat machine, shredded paper strips below, cream and charcoal palette, desaturated print on aged newsprint, flat graphic style
beats.video_prompt: pages slide steadily into the machine's slot while thin paper strips drift down beneath it, slow constant motion, no camera move, flat collage plane

beats.narration_text: Some postings were never real jobs. Some companies could never sponsor you anyway. And screening software rejects much of the rest before a human reads a word.

production_viz.label: what the machine deletes
production_viz.mechanic: a grid of application squares; three waves strike through subsets as each cause is named — 'never real' / 'no sponsorship' / 'auto-rejected' — serif labels, no percentages on screen (two-number cap)
production_viz.note: qualitative by design: ghost-job and auto-reject rates exist in the chapter but are excluded from the screen by the candidate card's two-number cap

beats.new_visual_element: [ISOTYPE] three named waves strike the grid

viz.label: deleted before a human reads it

beats.narration_text: Cold applications convert at roughly zero point two percent. Five hundred meticulous applications. That's one.

production_viz.label: ~0.2% — cold-application conversion
production_viz.mechanic: 500 pale outline squares count up fast (lag 0.003); when the grid completes, exactly ONE square fills ink — the arithmetic is drawn, not asserted (0.2% of 500 = 1)
production_viz.note: verify: ~0.2% cold conversion — chapter footnote carries its own [verify] flag; see FACTCHECK.md

beats.narration_text: Meanwhile, about fifty-four percent of hires — more than half — came through a personal or professional connection. A channel with no counter at all.

document.quote: 54% of hires came through a personal or professional connection.

beats.scene_description: the 54% set enormous in charcoal serif with the claim beneath; gold highlighter sweeps 'personal connections' on the spoken words — number 2 of 2

beats.narration_text: So why does anyone keep spraying? Because the good channel pays back on a delay. Nothing for days — then a conversation, then a referral, then an offer.

production_viz.label: two feedback clocks
production_viz.mechanic: two horizontal timelines: top (terracotta, 'the counter') sprouts a small tick instantly with every submission; bottom (blue, 'connections') stays silent for most of its length, then lands three widening blue marks — conversation, referral, offer
production_viz.note: qualitative timelines; no time units printed

beats.new_visual_element: [DIAGRAM] instant ticks vs the long quiet payoff line

viz.label: feedback now vs return later
viz.note: 12 instant ticks vs 3 late payoffs — illustrative

beats.narration_text: Your instincts read the silence as failure and the ticking as progress. So the hours flow to the channel with the loudest feedback and the lowest return.
beats.scene_description: the terracotta counter returns, smaller; hour-blocks slide toward it from the quiet blue line; the editor's pen rings the counter in terracotta on 'loudest feedback'

production_viz.mechanic: counter redrawn small, hour-blocks drift toward it, HandRing lands on the counter timed to 'loudest'

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the editor's pen rings the counter

beats.narration_text: The fix is not more effort. It's reallocation — moving the same hours to where the return actually lives.

production_viz.label: same hours, different channel
production_viz.mechanic: a stack of identical hour-blocks under the terracotta counter column slides, block by block, to the blue connections column; nothing is added or removed — the count of blocks is conserved on screen
production_viz.note: conservation is the honesty rule: same number of blocks before and after

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] hour-blocks migrate from terracotta to blue

beats.narration_text: The counter will keep offering you its number. Now you know what it measures: activity. The returns live somewhere quieter.

card.copy: Spend effort where the return is — not where the feedback is.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — the reallocation principle in one line
